paragraphs.body: My name is Rachel Mirell, and I am a freshman at the University of Pittsburgh. Recently in one of my classes we read “Girl,” written by you in 2003. This is a timeless rant that I think will never go out of style. I personally was at first confused by this essay. The formatting threw me off as well as how it made me feel. After re-reading, I realized this is all about the cramped feeling you experienced from learning what to do in your culture that was forced upon you by your mother who had the same ideals forced on her by her mother. I noticed how effective your use of repetition was for this essay. For instance, the repetition of becoming a slut and being a good housewife stood out to me because it was mentioned a few times and made me have to go back and read again to understand. What I concluded was that anything you did out of line from what you were told was going to be immediately criticized and you’d be outcasted. The description of the two extremes of being a religious housewife and a dirty girl and a slut seemed to contradict one another. I liked that you made it seem like a nagging voice in the back of your head telling you what to do. During my reading, I was able to make my own connection to this essay. I also grew up learning about what the norms are supposed to be for me and what to follow. I remember going to church every Sunday, learning what a lady is supposed to do, and how to balance cleaning, cooking, and going to school. While not the same as you it is similar. Both my parents work, and my mom was constantly teaching me and my sisters what we are supposed to do and how to act. Another example is when I was around 12 years old my friends started wearing makeup, so I tried some makeup, which was just mascara and lip gloss. I came home one day from my friends wearing the combo and was scolded by my parents who told me I was a whore for wearing what was meant for sluts. My parents are fine with it now they just didn’t want me to grow up too fast. So, I understand the confusion of doing something out of line changes the perspective and view of yourself. What I do wonder is why you decided it was best to write this essay in one big sentence and not break it up? I understand your anger, but why couldn’t you get it across with periods involved? Could it be that you needed it to be unhinged, or could it be that you wanted the confusion? If so, that really bothers me and I’m not a fan. Anyways, thank you for creating an essay that I bet so many can relate to in some way as I did. It shows we aren’t alone in what we face because everyone experiences similar things in different ways. This makes me more aware of what others face and that sometimes people just need to get things off their chest about their life.

annotations.text: essay. The formatting threw me off as well as how it made me feel. After re-reading, I realized this is all about the cramped feeling you experienced from learning what to do in your culture that was forced upon you by your mother who had the same ideals forced on her by her mother.
annotations.comment: I really feel as though this is at the heart of your communication with the author--could you be more specific? What about the formatting threw you off? How does that formatting make you feel cramped?

annotations.text: ther example is when I was around 12 years old my friends started wearing makeup, so I tried some makeup, which was just mascara and lip gloss. I came home one day from my friends wearing the combo and was scolded by my parents who told me I was a whore for wearing what was meant for sluts.
annotations.comment: This is an excellent anecdote to include in a letter to an author--this will give Kincaid a very strong sense of who you are and how formative your relationship with your parents has been.

You're doing really nice work here. The anecdote you share with the author is very personal and brutally honest in the middle of a strong academic discussion of the author's own work. I honestly would love to see you clarify some of your ideas about formatting. Ultimately, this is a really strong letter.
